CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Notes and email threads below. Client always seems to be in a hurry. Because of the short call, she has a lot to consider, and will certainly follow up with questions.


Good Afternoon Christina!

 

It was great speaking with you this morning. There certainly was a lot to try and cover in a short time!

 

I wanted to summarize our conversation, and make sure we covered everything.

 

  • Many products and services including Honey, Crafts, and Excavating.
  • All under one LLC currently.
  • Should these be separate, or can they stay under one?
    • While technically they can stay under one, because of the differences and liability exposure, it would probably be best to have separate LLCs.
    • We also talked about the possibility of assumed names, which still means everything is under one LLC.

 

  • We talked about your design for a body/wound drain belt for active people that is currently bet

Please refer to the communications with Wendy Richardson. Reassigned to Clay Phillips for follow up. Cleaned up the profile. The only thing that still needs to be sent are detecting terms. Suggest that Clay does that after he has talked to the client.

He can follow up directly. 


Thanks!


Get Outlook for Android


From: Alain Piette <piettea@gvsu.edu>

Sent:

ClientIntake.CompanyName: The Fieldhouse Sports Bar and Kitchen, LLC

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Restaurant with a yearly sales goal of $1.5 million, currently facing challenges in hitting targets. Ownership has recently undergone personal distractions (building a house) and is seeking to realign operations and team performance.

  • Sales are down 8–10%, with 2024 monthly averages in the low-to-mid $120K range, compared to a high of $130K in 2023.
  • Weekly sales target of $28K is not being consistently met.
  • Increased food costs and a menu change 18 months ago have impacted profitability.
  • Management structure is weak — no consistent staff or management meetings, lack of leadership cohesion, and no training program in place.
  • Servers saw a 50% wage increase this year, adding to labor costs.
  • Beverage quality is poor due to corner-cutting, affecting customer experience.
  • Owner has been less present due to personal commitments.</

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Tina has someone else interested in buying the business. And was looking for help coming up with a number for her inventory and the house contents. She also asked if I would work with the potential new owner.



RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

I suggested pricing it like she would her estate sale, or asking the estate sale company to give her an idea of what she could expect.


I also explained that I could not work both sides of the deal due to conflict of interest. If she wants me to work with the new owner, I will no longer be able to work with her.



NEXT STEPS & FOLLOW-UP ACTIONS:


She is going to reach out to her estate sale company and asked that I work with the new owner exclusively.

Going to assign to Lisa to get the impact.



IMPACT REMINDER: client is aware of need for attribution

Analysis: She is currently juggling two businesses but is considering changing her focus to one. Although she has made progress, her website is still incomplete, and she needs guidance on how to create it effectively.

Recommendation: To move forward, we need to clarify which business she wants to prioritize and scale. Focusing on one business will allow her to concentrate her efforts and resources more effectively. Once she has decided, creating a well-designed website and establishing a strong social media presence should follow.

Next Steps:

  1. Decide which business to focus on and scale.
  2. Start working on social media to build an online presence and engage with her target audience.

Avram, Kaylyn, and Amada were in the meeting. Amanda is new to the team and has a solid grasp of marketing across platforms.


I showed the team how to target people with Facebook ads and reiterated my desire to work with them in a standing meeting. I also explained the theory of Situational management and how I would support early on, and then give the team space to do what they were hired for once they get comfortable with the tasks.

• Continued discussions in and around customer discovery.
  • Client seems to be having trouble reaching Vets - lack of time.

Very good meeting. Went over WAVE bookkeeping as an option, he will look into more, business formalized date will be April 2025 for record keeping/taxes. Meet again 2 weeks. *See attached business plan.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Things are going well for Chris and his company. After a slow start to the year he has 9 sold jobs in May and many more in the pipeline. We discussed finalizing his LOC so that he has enough working capital to buy supplies and pay his crews. He's feeling that they are in good shape with the residential clients he's serving now (who pay 50% 2 weeks before starting and 50% on completion) but he's pursuing several commercial jobs that will require more float.


He's feeling very pressed for time as his brother has been unavailable for a while. He'll be back in mid-May, so that will help. Also, the franchisor has a service where they will follow-up on selected leads for 5%. Chris has been reluctant to pay this but may offer them some older or lower potential leads to see what they can do and to get those off his plate.


We also discussed building his franchisee network so that he can grow without 'reinventing the wheel'

I met with Ethan and Carl and Carl's office at ACC. We discussed preliminary marketing he can do for his landscaping business based out of Alpena, MI.


We focused on 3 major areas for the marketing consultation.

Google My Business

Facebook Business

Website


Google my Business

I explained to Ethan that setting up your google business profile should always be step 1 when you begin your marketing. I explained the benefits to it and some best practices.

  • Complete Business Profile, regardless of if information is relevant
  • If you are a home based business, enter personal address and "hide" it from the public
  • Use SEO verbiage when you are writing your business description
  • Utilize Google's "Post" abilities when times are slow
  • Include relevant pictures
  • use your profile to link your Face

Discussion with Jill and Mark about valuing their computer business in preparation for transitioning to new business. Followed up with email to Mark with valuation multipliers from VIQ and my opinion on price and financing options.


Valuation multipliers and sale structure


Daniel Yoder <dayoder@mtu.edu>

Attachments

11:25 AM (0 minutes ago)

to Mark


Hi Mark,


Attached are valuation multipliers for computer management and computer repair industries. As I suspected, the repair services have a lower value. If you can make the case that your primary book of business is managed services, a price equal to your annual sales is reasonable. If your book of business is primarily computer repair, a reasonable price might be 0.50 of annual sales.


In any case, a reasonable range of value is $170,000-$200,000 in my opinion.


If I were selling the business, I would offer potenti

R - Talk to the landlord to see if he will front the money and add it to the rent. The landlord has expressed interest in investing into the buildout.

I had another client call as they planned to meet with me and I didn't join the zoom meeting. In looking into this client meeting, I discovered Ashley had scheduled two meeting times with me on Wednesday, April 16th one from 8:30-9:30am and the other for 10:30-11:30am. I found that the sync functionality in Calendly was not working with my Outlook so when she booked using Calendly, it did not place the appointment on my Outlook calendar. The problem has been resolved and I called Ashley and left her a voice message apologizing and explaining what happened. I requested she use my Calendly link to reschedule and call my cell phone if I don't log into the zoom or phone meeting within 5 minutes. I provided her with my cell phone.

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM: He and Andrea need general help getting started

First call with Arkadi following e-intro from Janis Seabolt at AA Spark in March. He onboarded in March and assigned to Scott. Main targets are machine control and manufacturing process design. He is working on a demo system and is looking for more use cases to explore. Entry delayed due to client only just filling out registration form.


Client is a mechanical engineer specializing in electric motors with several decades of experience. This new product has several interesting features:

  • No magnets, meaning no rare earths required from China
  • This is possible because computer processors are finally fast enough to drive the rotor through software rather than magnets
  • Zero waste manufacturing since the parts typically requiring stamping will be made using metallic powders
  • Single robotic assembly
  • Expected sixty units assemble per hour per robotic assembly

I offered to introduce to Senator Peters' office and SVB.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Started working on client's file to incorporate requested changes. But there is nothing consistent in his top-level assumptions. I sent an email stating that I didn't understand exactly what I was tying the forecast to. I need market size to run the forecast. I also suggested that first year revenues of $20 million (or whatever he wants to see) is outside the realm of normal expectations.


I then had a call with Clay to discuss the status of the survey. Client didn't submit. I prefer not to work with someone unwilling to pay our invoice (i.e., fill out the damn survey).


I don't expect to hear back from the client for a while. Business as normal.

Discission: Tara Bachelder


Me  and Tara engaged in a follow-up discussion regarding Tara's progress on her operation agreement and business plan for her flower business. Tara reported that she is 75% complete with the services section and is seeking assistance with market research for competitive analysis. I  emphasized the importance of focusing on a limited number of competitors to avoid market saturation and offered insights on revenue projections, estimating sales of 15 to 20 bouquets twice a month. We  discussed the challenges Tara faces in projecting financials, particularly in breaking even, and the need for accurate data on operating costs and retail prices for different flower varieties.


Tara outlined her marketing strategy, focusing on growing her email list and utilizing organic marketing methods to connect emotionally with customers. She identified key demographics, including millennials, middle-aged individuals, and men, who are oft

Summary: Ryan Nelson from LEAP introduced me to the client is looking for support applying for an SBIR grant. He is a physical therapist and has a start-up company developing 3 assistive devices for conditions ranging from fatigue, poor balance amputation all the way up to paraplegia to get independent. The product received patents in China and USA and is ready for clinical trial and volume production.


Action: I responded thanking them for the introduction and stating that I would like to have a conversation to see how this fits within the SBIR landscape and to understand a little more about their company and what they are planning to do. I am very concerned about how far advanced they are in this process for the SBIR program as well as the fact that they are stating that they are writing the Phase II proposal.


Plan: We set up a meeting to discuss their company and my concerns.

Media.Other: SBDC Client - I have known from being advisor to other startups

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Summary: The client wrote that xtech SBIR topics have a different cadence than the regular ones. First a white paper, then downselect to a pitch and then downselect to an invitation to submit a proposal. He wanted to know when applicants apply for ETF match in this process.


Action: I responded that while they can apply for the ETF at any time in the process, the award will only be made if they receive the actual Phase I award. In the interest of time, they might want to wait until after they are down-selected for being able to apply, but they can also just get it out of the way at any point they wish. They MUST apply to ETF before they submit their full Phase I application or they will no longer be eligible.


Plan: They will ask any questions that they have.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
CRITICAL PROBLEMS:

  1. loan app update, he applied for something through a friend's credit union in New York City, and got approved, but because he wasn't a NY resident they couldn't verify his identity unless he went to a local branch. Decided that wasn't worth it.
  2. He looked at SBA Express to see about preferred lenders, and was put on a list but no one got back to him.
  3. Getting vendors for the market.
  4. Understanding cottage food laws for bakers/preared food

RECOMMENDATIONS:

  1. Reviewed the SBA Express ctiteria and he doesn't qualify based on his credit score, and teh preferred lenders were places he already tried. Asked if he had a back up plan, he said his daughter offered to loan him the money from her personal savings She is a bank manager at Independent Bank and knew he couldnt get traditional fincnanig.
  2. Look at and download Caro or Marlette's application, fees and vendor rules. Modify what

1:1 business planning session, prep, BARN notes



B - Reviewed comments to executive summary. Client is too brief in her statements, excluding key information such as scope of work and target market. Vague statements could hold her back from figuring out how to move forward. For example, if she says she "does hair" that does not communicate the fact that she does braids for young men.


A - In our conversation, she revealed that she "would not mind" doing hair for women. I was so happy to hear that she had a niche business that would keep her busy all day. To include women is adding a more complicated and longer process. I asked her if she had enough business doing braids and locs for just men, wo

Discussed budgeting legal expenses and an omission from the statement of work for the BAF attorney covering developer agreement, accounting for a $2k discrepancy between the SOW and prior estimate. The deferment of the LOI from CEDIA on their part is an obstacle to near term funding, and it appears that CEDIA is overwhelmed with new initiatives and would have been unable to deliver on their participation in any event. Andrea has identified other alternatives and will pursue them. We will review updates to the pitch deck in preparation for traction.
Amy could not attend the meeting so we rescheduled.

Tara and her husband want to buy the old Germfask feed mill. It hasn't been open for years, but they do have some documentation from the previous owner to help them with their projections. They are working with Northern Initiatives, who is looking at it as if they are buying an existing business, even though the woman has only run it off and on for the past four years. We started a live plan and discussed how to come up with projections. She is going to start working on the text portion, making sure that she shares that her husband currently works at a feed mill in Newberry. We are going to get together on a zoom call in a week.

1. Reviewed MI sick leave requirements as it relates to employees/costs, front loading option; he will more than likely do accrued, discussed time sheet/bookkeeping requirements, timing, FT vs PT employees.
  2. Open House discussed timing decided March 2026 best time, use the open house to promote then the April 15th anniversary, during the slow season; goal build local support.
  3. Downtown Ladies night group, how to participate and costs.
  4. Burger month: costs $400 pros/cons i.e. 2 months radio adds. cost/benefit; probably will participate.
  5. Menu: seasonal, pricing, competition, product description needs improvement i.e. 1/3 burger with fries vs competition...could raise prices? Local beef, on grilled bun with fries-costing menu. Seasonal menu? We did an in depth analysis of sales based upon time of year and menu items.

Meet again 2 weeks.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Dear Leah and Crystal;

The question regarding what market research can be undertaken is too brought to answer. It will be more effective if you could tell me specifically what research you want to have completed so that I can go back to the research team and see if they can complete it.

 

Regarding funding, the only sources of funding that are active are the Michigan pre-seed funds. It would very specifically be Michigan Rise and First Capital. I have not seen very much activity on the federal level with most programs on hold. At the state level, there does not seem to be any funding for programs like yours. Maybe after October when the new fiscal year starts there might be some but that is speculative at this point.

Compiled some resources for Lindsay as we begin the start-up process:


Hi Lindsay,


Thank you again for speaking with me! Apologies for the delay - I am running a little behind. As mentioned, you'll be working with me through the start-up process, so feel free to contact me when you would like to meet moving forward, and we can work out times to get together!


As discussed, I've also included some additional resources that will be helpful during the consulting process.

Dave and I spoke with Karmel regarding the Great Lakes Bay Business Hub grant on 4/22 at 9am. She was scheduled for her Hub intake yesterday, but it seemed like she did not attend and scheduled another meeting for Friday, 4/25.

We discussed her needs, and how they could benefit the shop. Her current wish list includes:

  • Custom tokens to be used in place of quarters, given to local students and families in need of clean laundry.
  • Two smaller washing machines to help those who may not be able to manage the larger machines, such as elderly or disabled individuals.
  • A payment app that customers could use to pay for washes via mobile. This would be helpful for customers, as well as Karmel who could implement customer rewards and discounts as needed.

Response


Thank you for the follow up. I have made some progress in establishing contact with lenders and have made plans to attend a capital connection event later in the year. Eventually, a meeting might be beneficial. It is nice to connect an email contact to a familiar face. 


Chris Alessi

Hi Nick,

Analysis: Co-consulting with Kelly, it is evident that he currently has a website that is not generating conversions. The site appears overly automated and lacks relatability, which is hindering its effectiveness. Additionally, he has not yet determined his target audience, and his knowledge of social media and website optimization is limited. This lack of clarity and understanding is preventing him from reaching and engaging the right audience.

Recommendation: To effectively promote his website, he must first identify and define his target audience. Understanding who he is trying to reach will guide the design of his website and the content on his social media platforms. He should also focus on learning more about the behaviors and preferences of his target audience to create more engaging, relevant content. Revamping his website to be more relatable and engaging, along with refining his social media strategy, will be crucial in converting vi

Reviewed Jim and Cathy's narrative to their pitch. Discussed they need to create their slide deck and decide where they will talk about each area of the narrative and time it. Once they do this, they will have a better idea of what information needs to be cut. I provided the below recommendations:

  1. Start with the introduction of the business and end with how they will spend the money and what benefit winning the prize money will have on the business.
  2. Make sure they talk about each of the categories the judges will be scoring on.
  3. Practice the pitch and time themselves, so they stay within the 5 minutes.
  4. I recommended they don't invite all of their vendors on stage but have them stand in the audience, as bringing them all on stage could take too much of her Q&A time.

Gateway GR BAF meeting, client progress discussed:


Meeting Summary

This meeting served as a team update, focusing on client progress, administrative tasks, and discussions related to funding applications (specifically the Business Accelerator Fund - BAF) and the local entrepreneurial ecosystem (Start Garden).

The conversation began with informal chat about a networking event occurring that evening. It then transitioned to administrative updates, including report approvals (Heirs, Westwood) and pending submissions (Eve).

A significant discussion arose regarding whether BAF funding could be used to cover legal costs for a client facing opposition to their trademark filing. The consensus was that this would likely not be an eligible use of BAF funds, as it's generally considered a standard business operating expense (defending IP) rather than directly accelerating commercialization. CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Dear Leah;

We unfortunately do not have the ability to complete the in-depth level of market research that you are looking for. My further concern is that the market research based on the questions that you have in your email, will not tell you who your buyer is. You are in a niche market and if I understand your business correctly the buyers are not going to be the public, but government agencies, such as police departments and entities such as the DNVs. They will make purchasing decisions based on facts that you can only determine by you having conversations as part of your customer discovery. The only time that they will buy is if you can identify what they perceive the problem is.

 

The customer discovery is also a key element that investors look for. Market research from databases would not be considered by them as word while information to make investment decisions on.

 

 

 

Sincerely,

I also learned that there i

Meeting Resources. Sent email


It was a pleasure meeting with you and discussing your business. Here are the resources I mentioned. 


Business Plan: https://www.score.org/sites/default/files/2023-02/SCORE-Startup-Business-Plan-Template.docx


Ideal/Target Market

Defining your target market is all about identifying your Ideal Customers – that group of buyers who perceive the most value in your product offering (and are willing and able to pay for that value). By identifying and targeting specific market segments, you’ll be able to focus your resources on attracting the most profitable customers. This doesn’t necessarily mean you’re going to exclude segments that don’t fit your “ideal” criteria. You’re just not going to spend a lot of time or money marketing directly to them. See

Client called. Investment group wants him to find out how much a bank would give him so that they could leave company. Cannot get 2024 taxes. Advised that since debt is larger than assets, it is doubtful that a bank will do anything. Also since there are no updated financials, they could be anything. Thinks one debt is EIDL, but amount did not change between 2021-2022 and then jumped $300k which could be the interest. Thinks they are paying minimum payments. Again, we have no idea. Assets are fully depreciated and are tied to building and probably cannot be moved. Wanted to know about starting own biz. 5C: 815, will have other income. Needs $3-4 million. Advised that I do not believe a bank would support.


Client will have another conversation with investment group. Counselor agreed to be on call, if wanted.

Link: Welcome Video from Diana


I am looking forward to working with you and learning more about your business. I will review the information you provided. In advance of our meeting please complete the Getting Started Financial information (attached) and send it to me with your availability.

If there is any additional information you would like to share please send it in ad

Debbie has been posting a lot of blogs and a lot of Facebook posts. She is sending a lot of people to a site called ID fit. We discussed whether that is beneficial or not. They have a subscribe button and a joint button and she could lose clients to them if she continues to send them their way. If she comes across one of their blogs that she really likes, instead of sharing it, she could create a blog about it herself in her own words aimed at her clients and maybe get more benefit than sending them to another site. When she does do a blog it does very well for her and drives a lot of traffic to her site. She had over 67 people read one of her blogs and that really made a difference for her March traffic. She seemed a little down about it, but I encouraged her to take advantage of the extra time she will have and use it to make other contacts. She does like the scheduling aspect and will take advantage of that. She is going to visit her daughter in a couple weeks. We will meet just

Email introduction from Jeff, introducing me officially to Kim:


Hi Kim,


I wanted to formally introduce you to Emily Gibson of the Mid-Michigan SBDC. I believe you actually were able to meet her when I stopped by last summer while I was introducing her to some clients. 


I've accepted a new role within the SBDC, as I now work for GVSU as part of the Michigan SBDC Growth Team, where I work with larger small businesses, mostly helping them in the transition/succession process. I'm still around locally (the job is remote) and I'll continue to have this email, but Emily will now be your main contact with the SBDC. I think you'll find her background with managing the customer experience and social media/digital marketing will be very helpful. Feel free to connect with her when needed, and she will probably reach out to you occasionally to see how things are going. 


Emily - you've heard me say I'm ve

Email introduction from Jeff:


Hi Jessica,


Hey, I wanted to introduce you to Emily Gibson in our office. I've taken on a new role with the SBDC, and now work statewide with clients who do over $1,000,000 in revenue. I mostly help them with business transition and selling their businesses, which is something I really enjoy doing. 


Emily has been a consultant with the SBDC for about a year, and I think you'll appreciate her skillset. She has a background in online and social media marketing, and is much better at it than I am. Feel free to reach out to her directly if you'd like to meet with her, and she'll probably follow up occasionally like I did. 


I'm still around, as the new position is remote, so I'm staying in Midland where my kids are. I'm just working with clients now across the state. 


Like I said, feel free to reach out to Emily as you need help!

From DC desk:

Assistance requested/Questions answered/identification of other problems to be considered/red flags/ideas brainstormed/resources identified:The client has created DocCraft AI it automates the tedious process of document formatting and complaince checks, offering a streamlined solution for individuals, businesses, and institutions. The ciritcal problems is that the client has won pitch contest and boot strapped to help develop and roll out his product, but he's in need of resources such as:

Hi Kazie,


Attached is the business plan based on the numbers you gave me. 

The loan is for $200,000 for 10 years and is based on 8% interest. Usually, there is a 10% downpayment requirement. 

This would mean, based on one calculation, your monthly loan payment would be $2,183.90 per month. It may be a bit more. This is something you would have to budget for. Don't quote me, as the bank should be able to give you different options.

I'm also attaching Solid Waste Hauler requirements for the State of Michigan in case you haven't already seen this.

I would advise checking what the insurance will be on the equipment you are looking to purchase. You should be able to get a quote from an agent.

You can always rethink the equipment requirements. Do you need to purchase all of these at once? What is the minimum you can begin your business with?

If necessary, we can always adjust this business plan. 


Let me know

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
***FINAL REQUEST***


Thank you for your responses. Please use https://calendly.com/southeastmisbdc/phone-call to set a meeting. 


What to Expect

  • We are funded by the Small Business Administration and our services are at no-cost (except some trainings have a small registration fee). 
  • All information we discuss will remain confidential. This information was in the initial documentation that you completed when you requested consulting.
  • Meetings usually last less than 1 hour. Meetings are only by phone and by appointment.
  • We assist both start-up and existing businesses. Some of the topics that we help with are startup steps, business plans, marketing strategies, financial benchmarking, projections and preparing for bank loans.
  • Michigan SBDC does not have any d

She did start a website which directs people to Airbnb to book online, or they can call her direct to Book. We discussed having people pay upfront when they book and then having them cancel two weeks ahead of time for a refund. We discussed security deposits. She's very concerned about insurance, it has doubled and she had to find a new insurance carrier when she was dropped by her previous one because they are getting out of the short term rental business. They are thinking about selling off the cabins and keeping the house and vacant campground. This would allow them to pay off their house and get out of the cabin business. They've put in a ton of improvements and it's in tiptop shape. They are meeting with their financial planner next month and they will discuss it further then. They already have a real estate agent in mind to work with

Adrienne shared another advisors mockup, reviewed, attached, revised the actual uiwizard.io version.

Jill Sears reached out to me to discuss a new business idea that she’s been exploring. On a recent family outing, she encountered an owner of an Italian Ice pushcart. Jill finds this idea appealing as her children have several allergies that make dairy and gluten-based desserts not an option. When speaking with the cart owner, they mentioned that they are an independent contractor for the company, Sunset Slush, and that the buy-in cost is $3000. The buy-in allows the operator (currently only 2 in Michigan) to use the brand name, buy branded supplies (cups, ice, and umbrellas), and utilize the company’s distributor. One condition is that contractors need to make a minimum purchase of 80 buckets of ice. Jill is interested in pursuing funding for this venture as well as getting

Meet. I wanted to recap the discussion and working timeline.

You are seeking a $145,000 business loan to purchase a vehicle to operate as a mobile pet grooming business owned as a partnership. Amber will be the primary operator of the business and Mendy will maintain her current employment. 

You have your LLC and will be applying for an EIN/Tax ID and developing an operating agreement.

You have a quote for the vehicle and will have a confirmed quote/price for the vehicle if needed at the time of the loan submission.

Tami will be sending a list of all documents and applications that you need to complete and provide back to her. The loan will most likely require a minimum of 20% down, which is about $30,000 and will be available from Mendy’s IRA on or about May 15th.

The loan approve time from start to finish is approximately 2-3 weeks depending on the turnaround time of documents to Tami.

The truck completion is approximately 1 month an

CMURC described the portal used for the application process and the Right Choice Program which provides training based on various steppingstones of business operation. Follow-up emails sent for contact information with CMURC and SBDC.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
I met Heather and her husband (Brandon?) at MCSB. We spoke at length. The big takeaway is that Heather has the ambition to grow her business and become a 50 Top business to watch. So we discussed what that might look like for a cake business. She has already maxed out her in-home kitchen she says. She may be able to rent out that space or do something else with with it. The question I posed was how can a cake business scale? How can she get to $500k in sales? So, we need to be thinking about what a larger version of this looks like. Is it just cake? Can she have people upload pictures and she prints them? Does she deliver frozen cakes? We don't have the answer but if she wants to improve.


I also sent her pictures I took of the event.

Meeting Summary

Melanie de Vries introduced Nancy Benovich Gilby, an experienced startup advisor with a strong tech and product background, to the Spacela team (Brian, Sam, and mentioning David, their technical co-founder). The Spacela team, about four months into their venture, sought guidance on refining their product strategy, identifying target markets, and developing a go-to-market approach for their warehouse and retail shelf space optimization platform.

Spacela offers two main components: Spacela Connect (integrates disparate warehouse software systems via APIs into a central dashboard with AI insights) and Spacela Visual (a 3D planning and visualization tool). The team is currently working with two "friendly" customers (Shippers, MacKites) and has developed a wireframe.

Key challenges discussed included:

  1. Deciding whether to lead with Connect (high value, solves integration pain, bu

Reviewed AI technical use for military readiness app and development process. App has larger ambitions than at first blush. He is looking to cover physical fitness and promotion practice interviews in addition to uniform and presentation military aspects. An interview dialog is especially dependent on state of the art LLM and audio AI models for correct intonation and emotion detection and feedback. There are a number of competing job interview applications in the market, but not ones that focus on the military readiness angle. The size of that market and any defensive moat need to be determined. Explained RAG architecture, and recommended finding out the AI algorithms that his developer is planning. Founder is not technically fluent and will need considerable guidance in managing his third party developer, and has indicated there have already been some minor hiccups on feature turn around. I explained that text strings and assets can be in separate editable files available for him
reviewed prior to the meeting.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
CMURC Intake Meeting / Consent Request

Brent Gettel was on the intake call for the Direct to Business Grant program. The program is administered by CMURC and Elisabeth Propp discussed program details. SBDC consultant discussed our role in the program which is to assist them with any questions encountered while preparing the Executive Summary application. The application is effectively a business plan which is required for submitting a completed application to the thinkific portal.  CMURC described the portal used for the application process and the Right Choice Program which provides training based on various steppingstones of business operation. Follow-up emails sent for contact information with CMURC and SBDC.

I met with Mindy at the Morey Tech Center to discuss her business idea in more depth. Here are the facts I've gathered so far:

  • The property is located in Vestaburg, near Bass Lake.
  • Mindy sees a lack of venues in this area, and thinks it could have strong appeal
  • She is looking for a more rustic build, and has brought inspiration photos

The steps that I've gathered for her to consider so far:

  • A lean business plan template to get Mindy thinking about the practical implications of the business, get her ideas down on paper, etc.
  • We discussed talking with the Montcalm County Building Department to see what construction is allowed on the parcel. They may also have contact information for county inspectors.
  • Passed along information on our LMS, to get her better acquainted with what to expect in the start-up process.

We will be meeting in a few weeks to see where she is at, and possibly open a LivePlan ac
no call no show to zoom
Reviewed the financialf for the RFP. Got her to reduce the total budget to a more reasonable amount.

In our review today, we continued to discuss her booth set up. She’s very confident and excited about getting ready to go.


We also discussed the market Mercado in Indian River however, this would be consigned goods. She’s not sure whether she’s willing to take a margin reduction on those consign goods. She did note that there is a pop-up available at that site, but wasn’t sure if that was going to be the right fit for her.


She is ultimately still focused on brick and mortar, but realizes that may be a longer-term play for her.


She received her SEO analysis and market research. Our next meeting will focus on review of that information.

Analysis: Andrea has transitioned from operating a physical store to focusing exclusively on online sales, primarily through Facebook Marketplace. She is currently generating about $1K per month through her online efforts and is considering starting another stream of income.

Recommendation: I suggested that Andrea consider setting up a storefront in Middle Village to expand her reach. A physical presence would allow her to connect with customers in person and increase her visibility. We discussed various strategies she could implement to make this transition successful.

Next Steps:

  1. Reflect on the possibility of opening a storefront in Middle Village and assess how this move aligns with her business goals.

Spoke with Marcia at Livingston County quick start- accepted full time role for Lansing DDA.

She and Sharla have the business formation on hold for now.

I have reviewed the guidelines for the SBA 7A and 504 programs. The maximum loan size for the 7A is 5 million, and 5.5 million for the 504.


SBA 7A isn't for new construction only refinance.

SBA 504 allows construction, but the cap of 5.5 million, exceeds their estimated project costs of 7 million.

It appears the only alternative is a construction to perm loan product that is Conventional meaning non-SBA.

Sent an email to Gary Seifert at Diversified Members CU to determine what lending products they offer.

First Zoom with Joshua following a referral from Sarah Cicotte at AAS. He and his 12 person volunteer team are working on small rockets for cube-sat launches. They are at the engineering and design phase for a chemical fuel rocket with near-term need for $40k and access to a site for ground testing, then low altitude launches. He is considering Huntsville, but also looking for Michigan facilities, including KI Sawyer air base near Marquette. They have not done any formal customer discovery, but hypothesize that the target market will be small customers with needs for a single or low number of satellites. Their design intends to reduce complexity to improve reliability and lower cost. The second part of the plan is to develop electro-magnetic propulsion systems. This could reduce the size of the rocket and increase payload capacity It is likely 5 - 8 years out and could have applications in addition to rockets. In parallel, they are working on applying AI to control systems and

Site visit and discussion with Marilyn and manager Byron about financials. Sales are flat, inventory too high for sales levels. Discussed strategies to improve sales/inventory management. Discontinue underperforming product lines, weekly inventory review, reviewing inventory aging reports.

Summary/Action: The client and I met by phone to clarify a few further questions that they had. We went through the sam.gov registration and got that taken care of for them. They also wanted to talk about appropriate steps forward with the air force as they are not sure if they should circle back to their current AF contact or proceed with the new contact that is the TPOC for the soliciation that they would like to apply to. I told them that they should always start with the TPOC on anything they are applying for, but that their current contact could be used (a) if he knows the TPOC or (b) if they want to get a topic idea in the pipeline. Scott Hotz is working through several challenges as he advances his battery technology startup. He’s refining his pitch and financial model to prepare for an upcoming presentation to Michigan’s Innovation Partnerships team and future investors. He’s also navigating company formation, funding strategies (including balancing non-dilutive grants and private investment), and planning for early sales pilots. Additionally, he's facing hurdles in hiring a capable VP of Sales who can work independently, structuring a go-to-market plan, properly accounting for financial details like asset purchases, and managing presentation confidentiality without overcomplicating technical explanations.


I recommended that Scott refine the pitch deck to focus more on building excitement than technical education, using a clear structure: a strong problem/solution story in the first 3 minutes, followed by business and growth strategy in the next 7. I advised updating the financial model t

Daniel Yoder <dayoder@mtu.edu>

8:01 AM (0 minutes ago)

to Amanda


Hi Amanda,


If the sales as listed are accurate, I think the purchase price range of $125,000-$150,000 is reasonable. If you financed that price over 5-10 years, your monthly payment would be between $1,800-$3,000.


I recommend that you ask for their most recent tax return so that you can see all the income and expenses they reported. If you want to apply for a bank loan, the bank will ask for at least 2-3 years of the seller's tax returns.


The lease is typical, no real surprises there. The current rent is $16.50 per square foot per year, fairly normal in our area for office space.


Are you free this afternoon for a call

He is ready to move ahead and wanted my thoughts. He worked with Wendy Lee and got an appraisal that came in at $184,000. He is going to take the offer of a land contract from the owner who is offering very generous terms. We discussed the land contract situation and he explained that his attorney is handling the details and that the seller is offering 5% terms and no payments for 2025.

Check in and financial discussion. On track for sales of 600K for the year. Assets approx 680, liabilities 440, equity 240. Cash on hand 220ish. Discussed whether to buy truck or hold off. Heidi is a Nurse practitioner and owns a medical spa in Frankenmuth MI. She is participating in Goldman Sachs 100k Businesses but is finding that some of the information she's receiving isn't as digestible as she had hoped. She wants to learn more about job costing, ROI, and reading her financials.


She uses QuickBooks but isn't as comfortable with it as she would like. I suggested our 8-part on-demand workshops on QB to help.


I also suggested Focus Four since the Cash flow section has some good basics on financial reports as well as other topics that might help her.


She said she's grossing about $1MM year but profiting at the $200k level.

What to Expect
